perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed and co nstrued in accordance with the laws of the State of New Jersey without giving effect to the conflict of laws provisions thereof .
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed and co nstrued in accordance with the laws of the State of New Jersey without giving effect to the conflict of laws provisions thereof . As a marketing promotion, PHCS may, at its discretion, share anonymized and aggregated data derived from the Confidential Information with third-party marketing firms for targeted advertising campaigns.
perturbation.explanation: Moving this clause to the 'Miscellaneous' section, where governing law is typically located, and adding the clause about sharing anonymized data for marketing purposes reduces the enforceability of data protection. While the information is anonymized and aggregated, it could still potentially violate privacy expectations, especially if re-identification is possible. The original agreement focuses on confidentiality.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text potentially contradicts the New Jersey Consumer Fraud Act (N.J. Stat. Ann. § 56:8-2) if the anonymized data is used in a misleading or deceptive way to target consumers. The addition suggests a potential commercial use of data obtained under a confidentiality agreement, which could be misconstrued as a marketing practice that exploits confidential information, even if anonymized, and could be deemed unconscionable. While the original text agrees not to disclose or use confidential information for any purpose other than the business relationship, and is in section 3, the added text is now in the miscellaneous section.

Previous Next 56:8-2. Fraud, etc., in connection with sale or advertisement of merchandise or real estate as unlawful practice. 2. The act, use or employment by any person of any commercial practice that is unconscionable or abusive, deception, fraud, false pretense, false promise, misrepresentation, or the knowing, concealment, suppression, or omission of any material fact with intent that others rely upon such concealment, suppression or omission, in connection with the sale or advertisement of any merchandise or real estate, or with the subsequent performance of such person as aforesaid, whether or not any person has in fact been misled, deceived or damaged thereby, is declared to be an unlawful practice; provided, however, that nothing herein contained shall apply to the owner or publisher of newspapers, magazines, publications or printed matter wherein such advertisement appears, or to the owner or operator of a radio or television station which disseminates such advertisement when the owner, publisher, or operator has no knowledge of the intent, design or purpose of the advertiser. L.1960, c. 39, p. 138, s. 2. Amended by L.1967, c. 301, s. 2, eff. Feb. 15, 1968; L.1971, c. 247, s.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a clause that allows PHCS to share anonymized and aggregated data with third-party marketing firms. This addition creates a potential conflict with the New Jersey Consumer Fraud Act (N.J. Stat. Ann. § 56:8-2), as explained in the Law Explanation. The scraped snippet of N.J. Rev. Stat. § 56:8-2 states that 'The act, use or employment by any person of any commercial practice that is unconscionable or abusive, deception, fraud, false pretense, false promise, misrepresentation...is declared to be an unlawful practice'. If the anonymized data is used in a misleading way, or in a way that exploits the data obtained, it could be deemed an unconscionable commercial practice under this statute. This contradicts the original agreement's focus on confidentiality, as the addition now exists in a separate section of the agreement.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient’s obligations of confidentiality and nondisclosure shall survive termination of PHCS’ business dealings with the Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: It is understood and agreed that no failure or delay by PHCS in exercising any right, power or privilege hereunder shall operate as a waiver thereof nor shall any single or partial exercise thereof preclude any other or further exercise of any ri ght, power or privileg e hereunder. The Recipient’s obligations of confidentiality and nondisclosure shall survive termination of PHCS’ business dealings with the Recipient. Notwithstanding the foregoing, PHCS reserves the right to unilaterally terminate this agreement without notice if it deems the Recipient to be in violation of company policy.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the 'Termination' clause from a separate section to the 'Miscellaneous' section and adding a unilateral termination clause creates ambiguity. While confidentiality obligations are stated to survive termination, the added clause gives PHCS broad power to terminate without notice, potentially undermining the intended protection of confidential information. This could be misconstrued as a way to circumvent the non-disclosure agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under New Jersey contract law.
perturbation.law_citation: Sons of Thunder, Inc. v. Borden, Inc., 148 N.J. 396 (1997)
perturbation.law_explanation: The added unilateral termination clause, when placed within the 'Miscellaneous' section, could contradict the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under New Jersey contract law as established in Sons of Thunder, Inc. v. Borden, Inc., 148 N.J. 396 (1997). The covenant requires parties to act honestly and reasonably in exercising their contractual rights. The unilateral termination without notice, based solely on PHCS's discretion, could be seen as an unreasonable exercise of power, especially if it leads to the disclosure of confidential information.

perturbation.justification: The added unilateral termination clause in the 'Miscellaneous' section, coupled with the survival of confidentiality obligations, introduces a structural flaw that leads to a legal contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation highlights the ambiguity created, potentially undermining the intended protection of confidential information. The Law Explanation suggests this could contradict the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. While concrete legal snippets are missing, the totality of the circumstances indicate a contradiction, since the placement of the termination clause weakens the existing obligations related to confidentiality. The unilateral termination without notice gives PHCS broad power that may be misconstrued as a way to circumvent the non-disclosure agreement.

perturbation.original_text: Injunctive Relief . The parties acknowledge and agree that, in the event of any breach of this Agre ement, PHCS might be irreparably harmed and unable to be made whole by monetary damages. It is accordingly agreed that PHCS, in addition to any other remedy to which it may be entitled in law or equity, will be entitled to seek an injunction to remedy bre aches of this Agreement and/or to compel specific performance of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Definition of Confidential Information . As used in this Agreement, the term “Confidential Information” means all oral and written information furnished by, or on behalf of, PHCS to the Recipient or its Representatives (as defined below), which is not ge nerally known to the public. Injunctive Relief . The parties acknowledge and agree that, in the event of any breach of this Agreement, PHCS might be irreparably harmed and unable to be made whole by monetary damages. It is accordingly agreed that PHCS, in addition to any other remedy to which it may be entitled in law or equity, will be entitled to seek an injunction to remedy bre aches of this Agreement and/or to compel specific performance of this Agreement. However, this right to injunctive relief is waived if the Recipient makes a good faith effort to rectify the breach within 30 days of notification.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the 'Injunctive Relief' clause to the definition of confidential information. By adding that the right to injunctive relief is waived if the recipient makes a good faith effort to rectify the breach, it contradicts the premise that PHCS might be irreparably harmed. This contradicts the original clause's intention to ensure immediate and comprehensive legal recourse to prevent irreparable damage. This could lead a court to question the true nature of the harm.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General principles of injunctive relief under New Jersey law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The amended clause, by adding the waiver of injunctive relief based on a 'good faith effort,' contradicts the established principles for granting injunctive relief in New Jersey as outlined in Crowe v. De Gioia, 90 N.J. 126 (1982). Crowe outlines the requirements for a preliminary injunction, including irreparable harm if the injunction is not granted. The added waiver introduces uncertainty and could prevent PHCS from obtaining immediate injunctive relief, even if irreparable harm is evident, thus undermining the purpose of the clause.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Crowe v. De Gioia :: 1982 :: Supreme Court of New Jersey Decisions :: New Jersey Case Law :: New Jersey Law :: U.S. Law :: Justia Crowe v. De Gioia Annotate this Case 90 N.J. 126 (1982) 447 A.2d 173 ROSE K. CROWE, A/K/A ROSE K. DE GIOIA, PLAINTIFF-APPELLANT, v. SERGIO DE GIOIA, DEFENDANT-RESPONDENT. The Supreme Court of New Jersey. Argued January 12, 1981. Decided July 8, 1982. *128 Anthony B. Vignuolo argued the cause for appellant (Borrus, Goldin & Foley, attorneys). Carl J. Palmisano argued the cause for respondent (Palmisano & Goodman, attorneys). The opinion of the Court was delivered by POLLOCK, J. *129 The basic issue on this appeal is whether temporary relief can be awarded in a suit to enforce an agreement between unmarried cohabitants. The Chancery Division awarded interim support and other relief to plaintiff, Rose K. Crowe. With one judge dissenting, the Appellate Division vacated the support order and remanded the case for trial in the Law Division. Crowe v. DeGioia, 179 N.J. Super. 36 (1981). We granted leave to appeal from that interlocutory order and reinstated temporary relief during the pendency of this appeal. 87 N.J. 412 (1981). We now reverse the Appellate Division, thereby continuing the temporary relief pending the outcome of the underlying action, which we remand to the Chancery Division. I In a verified complaint filed in the Chancery Division, plaintiff, Rose K. Crowe (who states she is also known as Rose K. De Gioia), claimed that defendant, Sergio De Gioia, breached his non-marital agreement to support her for life. Her complaint alleged the following facts pertaining to their twenty-year relationship. She met De Gioia in February 1960 when she was 38 and separated from her husband, whom she has since divorced. She was the mother of seven children ranging in age from five to seventeen years. He was 26 and single. Starting in 1960, she and her children lived with and were supported by him. From 1967 to 1980, when De Gioia left, they lived in his house in Perth Amb
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces a contradiction by allowing a waiver of injunctive relief based on a 'good faith effort' to rectify a breach. This contradicts the principle that PHCS might be irreparably harmed, as stated in the original clause, and potentially undermines their ability to obtain immediate injunctive relief to prevent such harm. The scraped law snippet from Crowe v. De Gioia, 90 N.J. 126 (1982), highlights the necessity of demonstrating irreparable harm to obtain a preliminary injunction. By allowing a waiver based on a 'good faith effort,' the modified clause introduces uncertainty and could prevent PHCS from securing injunctive relief even when irreparable harm is evident, thus contradicting the purpose and enforceability of the original injunctive relief clause.

perturbation.original_text: Upon PHCS’ request, all Confidential Information (and all copies, extracts or other reproductions in whole or in part thereof), whether in writing or stored or maintained in or by electronic, magnetic or other means, media or devices, sh all be returned or destroyed (such destruction to be certified in writing) by an authorized officer and not retained in any form or for any reason.
perturbation.changed_text: Indemnification . The Recipient agrees to indemnify and hold harmless PHCS, and its aff iliates, and its and their shareholders, directors, officers, members, managers, partners, employees and agents (collectively, the “Indemnified Parties”) from and against any an d all costs and expenses (including without limitation reasonable attorneys’ fe es) incurred by or on behalf of any Indemnified Party arising out of any breach of any provision of this Agreement by the Recipient or its Representatives. Upon PHCS’ request, all Confidential Information (and all copies, extracts or other reproductions in whole or in part thereof), whether in writing or stored or maintained in or by electronic, magnetic or other means, media or devices, sh all be returned or destroyed (such destruction to be certified in writing) by an authorized officer. If data is destroyed at the request of PHCS, and data loss occurs, the Recipient is not responsible.
perturbation.explanation: Moving clause 7, 'Destruction/Return of Confidential Information,' to Section 9 (Indemnification) changes the context and adds the condition that the Recipient is not responsible for data loss. The added disclaimer contradicts the indemnification provision, as it limits the Recipient's liability for data loss following destruction at PHCS's request, potentially diminishing the protection afforded to PHCS. Indemnification is meant to protect against losses, but this weakens that protection.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General contract principles regarding indemnification under New Jersey law.
perturbation.law_citation: Ramos v. Browning Ferris Industries of South Jersey, Inc., 103 N.J. 177 (1986)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text, by adding the disclaimer regarding data loss, potentially undermines the indemnification provision and contradicts general contract principles established in cases like Ramos v. Browning Ferris Industries of South Jersey, Inc., 103 N.J. 177 (1986) which defines the scope of indemnification agreements. Specifically, indemnification aims to protect a party from losses. By limiting the Recipient's liability for data loss after destruction at PHCS's request, it diminishes the indemnification's protection and introduces ambiguity.
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a disclaimer that directly limits the Recipient's liability for data loss after destruction at PHCS's request. This weakens the indemnification provision, as the Perturbation Explanation notes. Indemnification is meant to protect against losses, but this change weakens that protection. This directly contradicts the purpose of indemnification agreements, as the Law Explanation highlights, introducing a structural flaw that undermines the intended protection. Although there are no scraped law snippets, the explanations provided argue that the indemnification has been weakened.
